sound_type: rolling frog-like call -- low, resonant crrroo, more like an amphibian than a duck. Courtship display context. Diagnostic and unusual. E=4.
ecosystem: Wooded pond -- woodland ponds, beaver ponds, forested swamps, quiet backwaters. Sheltered, still, clear water surrounded by trees. Cavity nester requiring large tree cavities near water.
season: Year-round resident in the Pacific Northwest lowlands. Breeds in wooded areas near water. Winters on sheltered ponds and backwaters.
family: Anatidae (third consecutive entry: Harlequin 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120)

coordinates.description: Reference for wooded pond habitat in the Puget Sound lowland. Hooded Mergansers found in woodland ponds, beaver ponds, and sheltered backwaters west of the Cascades.

taxonomy_note.description: Third consecutive Anatidae entry. ONE-THIRD MARK (120/360 = 33.3%). North America's smallest merganser. Only member of genus Lophodytes. Fan-shaped black-and-white crest is one of the most dramatic displays in duck taxonomy. Rolling frog-like call. Wooded pond ecosystem. Cavity nester.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 3 entries (Harlequin Duck 118, Common Merganser 119, Hooded Merganser 120). Streak: 3 consecutive.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Lophodytes from Greek lophos (crest) + dytes (diver) -- 'crested diver.' cucullatus from Latin cucullus (hood) -- 'hooded.' The Hooded Merganser: the hooded crested diver.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Lophodytes -- monotypic genus. The Hooded Merganser is the ONLY member. Evolutionary divergence from Mergus occurred earlier than divergence between Common and Red-breasted Mergansers. Taxonomically unique.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes continues (third consecutive). The waterfowl order holds. After Piciformes (107-115, nine consecutive) and a brief Passeriformes return (116-117), Anseriformes has established its own streak.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae third consecutive entry. Streak: 118-120. Track continuation at 121. If 121 is also Anatidae, the streak reaches 4.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 400-2,000 Hz. Low, resonant rolling call. Most energy below 1,500 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 600-1,200 Hz. The rolling frog-like crrroo concentrates energy in the low-mid frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Male courtship call: rolling crrroo, 400-2,000 Hz. Female: hoarse croak, lower-pitched.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual call: 0.5-1.5 seconds. Rolling quality with trill. Given during courtship display sequences.
song_metrics.note_structure: ROLLING FROG-LIKE CALL: Low, resonant crrroo. The vocal membranes vibrate producing a buzzy, rolling tone. More like an amphibian than a duck. Accompanies crest display.
song_metrics.note_types: Male courtship crrroo (primary, distinctive), female hoarse croak, alarm calls. The courtship call is diagnostic.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual calls during display sequences. Not complex phrasing. Display-synchronized.
song_metrics.timbre: Rolling. Guttural. Frog-like. Mechanical. Resonant. Unlike any other duck vocalization. The rolling trill quality is diagnostic.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- moderate. Carries across wooded ponds effectively. Unusual quality rather than high volume. In sheltered pond environments, moderate amplitude suffices.
song_metrics.tempo: Slow to moderate. Calls given during deliberate courtship display sequences. Crest raises, head pumps, call emits.
song_metrics.context: COURTSHIP DISPLAY. Male raises fan-shaped crest, pumps head, emits rolling crrroo. Display-oriented. Also alarm and agitation contexts.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal documented variation. Display calls tend to be species-typical with limited learned variation.
song_metrics.key_property: ROLLING FROG-LIKE CALL -- low, resonant crrroo, more like amphibian than duck. E=4. ONE-THIRD MARK (120/360). Third consecutive Anatidae. Smallest merganser. Sole genus Lophodytes. Fan-shaped black-and-white crest. Wooded pond ecosystem. Still water. Cavity nester.

plumage.overall: Male: black upperparts, white breast, rufous flanks. Head black with enormous fan-shaped crest -- black at edges with large triangular white center. When crest raised, head appears to double in size. Amber-gold eyes. Female: brown-gray overall with warm rufous-brown crest (smaller than male's). Both sexes: serrated merganser bill.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 120: (1) MALE: Fan-shaped black-and-white crest (diagnostic, unmistakable when raised). (2) FEMALE: Rufous-brown crested head on gray-brown body. (3) SMALL SIZE -- smallest merganser. (4) AMBER-GOLD EYES (male). (5) WOODED POND HABITAT.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Males in eclipse plumage resemble females but retain amber eyes. Non-breeding season: crest less frequently displayed.
plumage.flight_pattern: Fast, direct flight with rapid wingbeats. White wing patches visible. Small, compact silhouette in flight.

physical_description.overall: Small merganser. Body length 40-49 cm. Wingspan 56-70 cm. Weight 450-880 g. Roughly half the mass of Common Merganser. Compact body. Fan-shaped crest. Short, triangular serrated bill.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 120: small duck on a wooded pond with a dramatic fan-shaped black-and-white crest (male) or rufous crest (female). If the crest raises into a fan shape and the bird is on a sheltered, tree-lined pond -- Hooded Merganser.
physical_description.field_id: Small merganser on wooded ponds. Male: dramatic fan-shaped crest (black edges, white center), black-and-white body. Female: rufous-brown crest, gray-brown body. Both: amber-gold eyes, small serrated bill. Rolling frog-like call during courtship display.

nodes.title: The Fan-Shaped Crest -- Dramatic Display
nodes.description: Male's fan-shaped black-and-white crest: one of the most dramatic displays in duck taxonomy. Head appears to double in size when raised. Courtship, territorial, alarm functions. Flat when relaxed -- completely different bird.

nodes.title: The Rolling Frog-Like Call
nodes.description: Low, resonant crrroo more like an amphibian than a duck. 400-2,000 Hz. Rolling trill from vibrating vocal membranes. Courtship display context. Diagnostic and unusual. E=4.

nodes.title: Anatidae Streak -- Three Consecutive (118-120)
nodes.description: Harlequin Duck (118, turbulent stream), Common Merganser (119, river), Hooded Merganser (120, wooded pond). Three ecosystems: rapids to river to pond. Three genera. The waterfowl entrance at the one-third mark.

nodes.title: Sole Genus Lophodytes -- Taxonomic Uniqueness
nodes.description: Only member of genus Lophodytes. Early evolutionary divergence from Mergus. Monotypic genus at the one-third mark. Joins American Dipper (Cinclus) as monotypic-genus representative. Unique taxa at structural boundaries.

nodes.title: Wooded Pond Ecosystem -- Still Water
nodes.description: Woodland ponds, beaver ponds, forested swamps. Sheltered, still, clear water surrounded by trees. Cavity nester. The quietest aquatic habitat in the engine. The water theme culminates in stillness.

nodes.title: Water Culmination -- Torrent to Stillness (116-120)
nodes.description: Five degrees of progressive calming: fast stream (116-117) to turbulent rapids (118) to river (119) to wooded pond (120). The full hydrological spectrum. The engine has traced the water from mountain torrent to woodland pool.

nodes.title: One-Third Mark -- 120/360 Taxonomic Summary
nodes.description: 120 species. All avian. ~25-30 families. Picidae nonet (107-115, largest streak). Anatidae streak (118-120, current). Monotypic genera at boundaries. Forest to fire to water to stillness. The engine's ecological trajectory documented.

cross_references.relationship: MERGANSER CONTRAST. Largest vs smallest. River vs wooded pond. Guttural croak vs frog-like call. Same tribe, opposite scale. Consecutive degrees.
ANATIDAE STREAK. Three consecutive: Harlequin (turbulent stream, E=5), Common Merganser (river, E=4), Hooded Merganser (wooded pond, E=4). Three ecosystems, three genera.
WATER CULMINATION. Five degrees: fast stream (116) to wooded pond (120). Full hydrological spectrum. Torrent to stillness. MONOTYPIC GENUS PARALLEL: Cinclus (116) and Lophodytes (120).
CAVITY NESTER LINEAGE. Woodpecker excavates in burned snags. Merganser uses existing cavities near water. Fire creates snags; decay creates cavities; mergansers nest in them.
CAVITY CREATION. Flickers and woodpeckers create the cavities that Hooded Mergansers depend on for nesting. The Picidae-Anatidae connection: one makes holes, the other uses them.
